sentences.text: It's 57 years since a Kristallnacht.
The death of my father changed my life.
In December, 1937.
I was a schoolgirl.
My father was sick in bed with a coronary.
And suddenly, at night, there was a knock at the door.
And they said there's police.
I didn't believe.
What are police doing with-- so I rang the police.
Imagine?
I was so young, and I had enough brain to ring the police if it's true that they sent somebody.
And they said, yes.
They came.
And they said that they want my father to get out of bed.
It was night-- and to come with them.
And I-- and we--
First-- first-- first we said he can't get up, because he had-- he is a sick person.
We can give all the certificates from all the doctors that say-- can't get up.
So then they-- it was going on like this.
I don't know for how many hours.
And then they said, apparently-- I-- I went to school.
Because he was staying-- stayed home.
And we had all the hope--
The next morning.
We had all the hopes that he will stay home because of the doctor's certificate and everything.
And, apparently, they said they need him as a witness for somebody, and it is essential.
And it won't interfere with his health.
And he will come back soon.
And that's how they got him out from home.
When I came back from school, I saw my father standing on a train stop with my brother-- I don't remember if my mother too-- going to the next city, to Sosnowiec.
And I didn't know what happened.
With police-- I think it was policemen-- as I-- I visualize this.
It was policemen.
Yeah, yes, yes.
And I didn't know what happened.
I came home.
And I-- I-- they said-- as I saw I-- all my-- my father's relative were in Sosnowiec.
And they took him to Sosnowiec there.
So I-- I-- I-- I-- I don't know how, or who gave me the initiative, or the-- the wisdom to go there.
But I did go.
And then I saw my-- my father sitting-- not in a jail, but like in a jail room-- and waiting there.
Where?
Where were--
In Sosnowiec.
In a police station.
It must have been a police.
It wasn't jail.
It must have been a police station.
And-- and I was very worried about him, because I knew-- they say that he had to go to Warsaw to-- to be witness there.
And it was a long, long trip to Warsaw.
So I decided on my own--
Maybe 15, 15 and a 1/2-- that I am going back to-- to Bedzin.
And I get his tallit, which was the most important thing for him-- to be able to pray-- and also that I get his-- his medication, the insulin.
He was a-- a sugar diabetic.
I was born, apparently, when he was already a sugar diabetic.
And I never got sugar diabetic.
So I went.
And I got those essentials for him, and took a taxi back to Sosnowiec.
When I came there, there were so many people there.
And they took my father out of this police station.
And we were going to the train station.
It was like a-- a live funeral.
What-- people had just come to watch and see?
People just came to say-- to farewell him somehow-- somehow.
Everybody was walking after my father.
And my brother went together with him in the train to-- to Warsaw.

And he thought that in Warsaw he'd be able to have some, you know, some connection to somebody who can-- who can verify that my father hasn't done anything, that he is-- that he is not guilty of nothing.
Because then we knew that they-- they will-- to carry-- take him to Kartuz-Bereza.
Kartuz-Bereza was the first concentration camp in Poland.
They used to take the really like communist, and people which were-- they-- they used to-- to treat the people there like in-- in labor camps.
The Polish of-- of Polish police.
A Polish--
Yes-- camp.
Yes.
And that's why my-- my-- my brother couldn't get an audition to anybody of those big-- big people, like rich [NON-ENGLISH] at that time, or whoever was at the-- at the top of the government.
And they promised him that he will be able to talk to them on Monday.
But in the meantime, they took my father away into the-- with-- with the police, which were pretty nice to him.
My brother said that they were cracking jokes even.
And the police told me then-- told us or told me-- that my father put on the tallit when he was close to Bereza.
And he was praying.
And then he complained that he had got-- got pains in his chest.
But they wouldn't believe him.
So they took him down from the Kartuz-Bereza train-- in Kartuz-Bereza, from the train, to a buggy and horse.
And in that buggy and horse, he was supposed to reach Kartuz-Bereza.
But when they got to Kartuz-Bereza, they tried to push him-- to take him down.
He-- he was dead already.
He never reached Kartuz-Bereza.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [CRYING] He must have been-- I don't know.
I think it must have been Friday night when he-- when he died, because I remember my mother was lighting the candle.
And one candle blew out.
And I got such a shock.
I remember this.
And then it was Saturday.
And the police came.
And they say, who is he?
They say oh, maybe my mother, an elderly woman already-- because as I told you, I was born when-- when they were nearly 40.
So they took-- my brother wasn't there.
And they took me to the police.
And I didn't know what for.
In the meantime, my brother found out what happened.
And he came to the police station.
And he went in.
And I could see-- could hear him crying.
So I knew already what happened.
And I cried.
I cried.
They took me again home on a buggy and horse.
And I don't think that I-- that I had-- that I had one breathe without crying.
I was screaming.
But when I got home, I fainted.
I remember this clearly.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that was-- that was hard.
It took three or four days that they could-- could get the corpse of my father back from Kartuz-Bereza to Bedzin.
I think one uncle from Lwow went.
And one uncle from Sosnowiec went.
And they brought him back.
And on a Tuesday was the funeral.
It was--
It was Hanukkah-- fifth day of Hanukkah when he died.
So I'd celebrate--
And I'd celebrate his [NON-ENGLISH] according to the fifth day of Hanukkah.
I just got yesterday a letter from the synagogue that it falls this year, on the 21st of December.
What can I tell you about the funeral?
The-- all shops were closed in the whole city.
They were taking my father from every synagogue-- from one synagogue to another synagogue.
The body.
The whole school, everybody-- I don't remember anymore anything.
But I know that it was a grief for the whole city.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And when the-- I remember when-- when we were sitting Shiva, I said-- I remember the kids were coming and trying to comfort me.
They-- they said that next week they are having an essay from-- from Polish, or that we're going for an excursion here and there.
And I remember clearly that I said, how can anybody make plans?
If anything like this could happen so sudden, you can't plan anymore.
So this-- this event, besides the-- the personal tragedy--
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I needed something-- I needed an ideal.
I needed something that I could believe in.
And I found a-- a-- a boy who told me how to become a great Zionist.
I grew up.
I grew-- anymore.
I didn't listen anymore to-- to-- to anybody.
Yeah.
The future-- I-- when I-- when I told my mother, you know, slowly and slowly, Vienna was-- Austria was occupied by the German.
Sudeten was occupied by the German in 19-- Vienna in-- in-- in March '38; Sudeten in March '30-- in September '38.
And I said to my mother, why don't you sell everything?
And we'll got to Palestine.
I said, what for?
I've got Palestine here.
This is what your mother said?
So you became very Zionistic.
I became very Zionistic.
And I-- I had a-- a boyfriend which was very Zionistic.
And we hoped one day to go to Palestine.
My mother was against it.
Oh, no.
No, my mother was against the boy.
My mother was against him.
But that boy followed me everywhere I went with my mother.
If I went to this place for holiday, or to this place for holiday, he followed me always.
And that how he followed me, even when I was running away when the war broke out.
And Sudeten [NON-ENGLISH] was taken.
And we all expected that the next will be Poland.
Will be the next-- definitely.

We didn't know when, and didn't know-- we even went in the summer of '39 with my mother to a very beautiful place, which was called [NON-ENGLISH] where my mother was you know, like-- my mother was having spas there and things like the--
In Poland.
My parents were-- went everywhere in-- in-- in Germany and in-- in Czechoslovakia.
They never went to America or to-- nobody went that-- they-- so far.
But in that summer of 1939, I had a ball there.
And my mother wasn't very well.
She had some trouble with some gallstones, I remember.
And it became so clear that the war is going to break out anyway, that we decided in the last minute to come home.
We came home maybe on the 30th of August, on the--
And the first of September, the war broke out.
And as we lived so very close to the German border-- it was maybe 16 kilometers to German border from us.
So we decided-- my mother had a brother in the center of Poland, a city called Piotrkow, which doesn't mean anythings.
And we decided we'd go there.
You and your mother were going to pack up and-- and-- and go to--
Pack-- we packed up and we went by train.
Piotrkow, yes.
And we stayed there.
And that boyfriend of mine followed us to there.
Shlomo Schiff.
Yeah.
We remained only a day or two, because we thought it'll take the German most probably a year to conquer Poland.
But it took him a day, and he was already nearly in Piotrkow.
So me and Shlomo decided we'll go-- we further.
And we were-- his family was somewhere not far from the border of-- of the Russia-- far, but much closer than-- than from there, from the Russian border.
We said, we're going to-- to meet his parents in Sanok.
Sanok was further east.
Further east.
And we-- we-- from there, we went further.
It took us 18 days.
We decided we're going to the Russian border.
She left.
She stayed in Piotrkow.
No, with her-- with her brother and his family.
She wasn't very happy.
But I said, I'm afraid.
And I'm not staying here.
Yeah.
His family too.
His family were fantastic to me, really.
Till today, his sister writes to me letters.
And we-- we went as far as the-- really, the border town of Poland and Russia--
--which was Ostrog.
18 Days.
We traveled by foot.
We traveled by buggy and horse.
We traveled by car.
We traveled by anything that was possible.
And we traveled.
And the bombs were falling down wherever we were going.
People are-- were dying.
And-- and-- and blood was spread everywhere where we looked.
Moving east.
But the big move east was after they occupied the heart of Poland.
You know, because it came in like this-- that when we got to Ostrog, which was the border city of Poland and Russia, suddenly we saw the Russian troops coming forward towards us.
And we couldn't--
Yes, and we couldn't understand what happened.
And then we found out that Hitler and Stalin make the pact that part of Poland will be occupied by the German, and part of Poland will be occupied by the Russian.
And as it happened, I was now under-- under the Russian occupation.
And my mother was under the German occupation.
What to do now?
That's right.
I was with the Schiff family.
But I also had-- as I told, you-- remember, that one of my uncles from Lwow went to collect the corpse of my father.
So I had an aunt and a-- and-- and-- and her family, my-- my-- my father's sister and her family, who lived in Lwow.
So we moved back to Lwow, and I--
From Ostrog.
We only stayed there a few-- a few days.
And I lived with my-- my aunt.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
By the-- I even wanted to go to uni in Lwow.
By the Russians.
And I must say that the-- I was under the Russians from September '39 till June '41.
And I must say, I was quite lucky there.
My brother came there, because he also crossed the border, because everybody wanted so that it's much better to be under the Russian than to be under the German.
Yes, to Lwow.
No.
By-- by--
Alone with another cousin.
You know, everybody was running.
In Bedzin.
My sister-in-law was in Tomaszow, where she comes from, with-- with my brother's child.
And me and my brother were in-- in Lwow.
We didn't live together, because he came with another cousin.
And he lived with the cousin.
I was knitting.
And he was selling what I knitted on the market.
I became a knitter.
I became a top knitter.
Would you imagine me, that I do-- couldn't do a thing?
But I only knew how to knit as a little girl.
They created a cooperative under the Russians.
And they took me there.
And I became the quickest.
I was the quickest knitter of 188 girls.
And because of this-- in Russia, I don't know if you know what it is-- a [NON-ENGLISH].
A [NON-ENGLISH] is somebody who excels in anything, who is the quickest in running, the quickest in knitting, the quickest, the best, the best at school, [NON-ENGLISH].
You are the best.
And I was the champion knitter.
And so I had to deliver speeches, which I didn't believe into.
But I had to speak the way they want me to speak-- on the first of-- of May, on the eighth of March, which is the Women's Day.
And you know, I-- I-- I-- I earned a lot of money that way.
And I was able to send my mother parcels.
I was all the time in-- in touch with my mother.
Post.
By-- by post.
Regular post, yes.
That continued, yes.
A great worker.
And I found a-- I-- I had a boyfriend.
And I had-- and he was studying medicine.
And he continued to study medicine at the Lwow University.
And he was-- as it happened, he lived in the same house as my aunt lived.
Ah, finished.
Finished, finished.
I don't want to talk about it.
Yeah.
Till the war broke out--
--till the war broke out again between Germany and-- and Russia.
Oh.
This-- there's no words that can describe the disaster that happened.
Ugh.

There were-- they were knocking on every Jewish door, dragging out the Jews, putting them-- killing them on the street, or putting them in-- in some big orphanages, or some-- then from that, taking them to labor camp, to concentration camp.
There were thousands, thousands of them dying every-- every minute and every day.
The Germans-- the Germans.
I was-- I was with my aunt.
And we were lucky.
They were knocking on our down.
And my aunt had a Ukrainian servant.
And the Ukrainian servant spoke Ukrainish to them.
And they thought that they-- we don't-- nobody lives here-- of-- of Jews.
There's no Jews here.
Antonia.
Antonia.
Yeah, yeah, at this-- at that particular-- because I got saved hundreds of times.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we had to move to the ghetto.
In Lwow.
I don't remember how.
This is a blank, completely blank.
I know that I've been in the ghetto.
How I got to the ghetto, I don't know.
I was with my aunt, and with my uncle--
--and with-- and with-- with her daughter.
Her son got taken to the army by the Russian, you know, while we were under the Russian occupation.
He was at the right age to go to the army.
We never heard from him anymore.
The ghetto was terrible.
We didn't have food to eat.
We didn't have-- we had nothing.
My-- my uncle was quite well off.
And he had some money-- some put away.
And he could help a little bit of buying this and this.
But I decided I go out of the ghetto looking for a-- for a job somewhere.
Maybe I can-- I can-- I didn't look Jewish.
I got out of the-- I just-- you know, it wasn't yet a ghetto that you-- you-- you had to pass something.
I just went out.
And I went.
And I found a job in a German laundry.
I was washing every day the washing that come-- came from the front-- full of lice, full of bacillus, full of dirt.
And I was-- there was no washing machine.
There was no dryers-- scrubbing like this.
And, of course, my hands got swollen.
My feet got swollen.
And I was-- I was really pretty, pretty bad off.
But I was young.
You know what it is when you are-- you're one day not well, and then the next day you're well.
And my-- and I was able to bring some bread home to my family too.
Outside the ghetto.
Returned--
--every night to the ghetto.
I don't remember-- no.
I most probably-- no, when I was going out of the ghetto, I wasn't wearing.
Illegally-- illegally going out of the ghetto.

I was illegally going to another city to give-- to bring food to the-- to the family of my boyfriend that lived there, to bring them news, even horrible news, that his brother and his sister-in-law got killed by the German.
And one day, when I came home from this laundry, there was a man there.
And he-- he handed me a little note.
And on the note was written, please trust this man.
And I recognize my mother's handwriting.
And my mother sent him.
She paid him most probably lots of money, because he was a smuggler-- to smuggle me back to her, because between me and my mother was a border.
Where I-- where I was, it was called the German protectorate.
Where my mother was was called the Drittes Reich.
And she thought, hearing what atrocities were going on in this part of occupied Poland, by the German, that I have more chance if I be with her.
So she sent this man.
And I trust him.
I left my aunt.
And I went with him.
Don't ask me how long it took, how dangerous it was, and how many times we were nearly caught.
But I reached my mother in December, 1941.
Yeah.
I returned back to Bedzin, to my mother.
And I met my mother in the street.
It was-- it was a fantastic reunion.
She somehow grew up too.
She wasn't the same as she was.
She wanted my love.
She didn't want me to be scared of her anymore.
She only wanted my love.
And my mother lived, at that time already, not in the center of the ghetto.
But she was thrown out from our beautiful home.
And she was living in somebody's kitchen, in the back of the kitchen, divided by a wardrobe.
And there I stayed with my mother.
In the ghetto-- not quite the ghetto, but outskirts of-- of the city.
In Bedzin.
That was some friends, my father's friends.
They always lived in this part of the city.
And they let her stay there in this part of the kitchen.
And there were my-- part of my furniture of my room in that part of the kitchen.
Jewish, Jewish.
Ernst was their name, I remember.
I came-- I came back to my mother.
I moved back to my mother.
But I was illegal there.
I had no identification papers.
I had no coupons for bread.
I had no coupons for-- for clothes.
I wasn't allowed to go out, because anybody ask me who I am, they-- they put me into jail.
I thought the worst thing what can happen is to go to jail.
I didn't know about extermination then-- camps.
I-- I remained hidden.
Sometime I used to go out with my girlfriends, you know, they hid me.
And I was nine weeks with my mother.
And one night, after the nine weeks-- knock on the door-- the police.
They took me away.
No.
No.
They took only me.
And they took me into a big-- that-- I remember that it used to be a orphanage, this hole that they took me there.
And there, I saw lots of people-- girls about my age, and older women, and men.
And straight away was a segregation-- like a girls my-- my-- you know, not married girls, and the married women, and the men till 40, and after 40.
And they segregated us.
Young women.

And they took us to the next city, the-- the one-- the-- the-- the young girls and the younger men-- the not married girls and the younger mens-- went to another city, to Sosnowiec again, where we went to-- like, into a camp in between.
